summary: Gruber dismantles Engadget editor Ryan Block's handling of a bogus internal Apple email claiming iPhone and Leopard delays. He argues the hoax should have been obvious because Apple had issued no actual press release — something required by law before such news could circulate internally. Gruber further criticizes Block's follow-up as self-justification rather than apology, and points out that Block didn't merely report what he knew (an internal email was circulating) but instead presented the delays as confirmed fact. The core failure was prioritizing a scoop over basic journalistic skepticism.
key_points: The hoax was detectable because the email referenced a press release that didn't exist — Apple is legally required to disclose such news publicly before or simultaneously with internal communication.
Block's follow-up framed his decision as responsible journalism, but Gruber reads it as rationalization driven by scoop anxiety rather than editorial judgment.
Block presented unverified internal email content as confirmed fact, using language like 'on authority' that implied executive-level sourcing he didn't have.
There was a defensible version of this story — transparently reporting that an internal email was circulating — but Block chose sensationalism over accuracy.
money_quotes: There is no way — none — Apple would first announce a dramatic change in iPhone availability via wide internal email. No way.
Apple cannot release news like this internally before issuing a public statement. It's not just good manners, it's the law.
The red flag here is the phrase 'issued a press release today' — which, you will note, is in the past tense. If they issued a press release, where was the press release?

I don't blame the Apple employees who received this email and believed it to be true, at least at first; they had no reason to be skeptical. But Ryan Block's job is to be skeptical of sensational news.
Translation: I jizzed my pants at the thought of having a scoop on this, and posted it because I thought some other site might post it first.
'On authority' implies just the opposite — that Engadget's source knew, firsthand, as in like executive-level knowledge, that this information was true.
I don't see how anyone can argue that what Block did post — which was completely and utterly false — was anything other than sensational jackassery.
key_insight: A tech journalist's job is to be skeptical of sensational news, and presenting an unverified internal email as confirmed fact — while obscuring the actual source — is not reporting, it's reckless amplification.
